cover_page.title: AIS Document Style Guide
cover_page.subtitle: Content Block & Typography Reference
cover_page.confidentiality: Internal Use Only — AIS Document Template Reference

content.text: This section demonstrates all five heading levels available in the AIS document template. Each heading level has distinct sizing, weight, and color to create a clear visual hierarchy. Section headings (Heading 1) are generated automatically from each section's heading property.
Heading 2 — Subsection Level
Heading 2 is the primary subsection divider within a section. It appears in the Table of Contents at the second level.
Heading 3 — Sub-subsection Level
Heading 3 provides a third level of hierarchy. Use for step sequences, procedure names, or detailed breakdowns within a subsection.
Heading 4 — Detail Level
Heading 4 is styled in medium blue (#125A97) at 12pt bold. Use sparingly for fine-grained organization within a Heading 3 block.
Heading 5 — Minor Detail Level
Heading 5 is the deepest heading level, styled in dark navy (#1C2C57) at 11pt bold. Use only when genuinely needed.

The first_paragraph type uses the FirstParagraph style, which provides a slightly different treatment for the opening paragraph of a section — typically without first-line indent and with appropriate spacing after the heading.
Normal Body Text
This is a standard body paragraph using the Normal style. It is the default for general prose content. Body text uses the document's base font (typically Arial 11-12pt) in dark gray (#3C3C3C).
Multiple body paragraphs flow naturally with consistent paragraph spacing. Use this type for explanatory text, descriptions, and general narrative content.
Compact Style
The compact style provides tightly-spaced body text with minimal before/after spacing (36 twips each). Use it for dense informational content where vertical space is at a premium.
Multiple compact paragraphs stack closely together, creating a dense but readable block. This is ideal for configuration details, specifications, or reference material.
Body Text with Bold Label
The BodyText style provides an alternative body treatment. When combined with the bold_label property, it creates a definition-list effect.
This pattern is useful for callout boxes, notes, warnings, or any content that benefits from a bold prefix.
Proceeding without proper authorization may result in access revocation.
Bold Blue Accent
The AISBoldBlue style renders text in bold with the AIS brand blue color. Use for key statements, section callouts, or emphasis that should stand out from normal body text.
The generator supports both bulleted and numbered lists at multiple indentation levels.
Bullet Lists
Use the bullet or list_paragraph type for unordered lists. Each item receives a bullet marker from the template's numbering definitions.
First bullet item — top level
Second bullet item with **bold emphasis** and *italic text*
Third bullet item with `inline code` formatting
Sub-item under the third bullet (level 1 indentation)
Another sub-item at level 1
Deep nested item (level 2 indentation)
Fourth bullet item — back to top level
Numbered Lists
Use the numbered_list type for ordered sequences such as procedures, steps, or ranked items.
Navigate to the Azure Portal and select the target resource group.
Click **Access Control (IAM)** in the left navigation pane.
Select **Add role assignment** and choose the appropriate role.
Search for the security group and select it.
Click **Review + assign** to complete the assignment.
Mixed Lists
Bullet and numbered lists can be interleaved with body text for complex procedural documentation.
Prepare the environment:
Install Azure CLI v2.50 or later
Authenticate with `az login --use-device-code`
Deploy the infrastructure:
Clone the repository from Azure DevOps
Run `terraform init` followed by `terraform plan`
Validate and verify the deployment.
Content blocks support markdown-style inline formatting within any text field. The following demonstrates all supported inline patterns.
Bold Text
Use double asterisks for **bold emphasis**. This renders as Word bold formatting. Example: The **Azure Resource Manager** deploys all resources through **declarative templates**.
Italic Text
Use single asterisks for *italic emphasis*. Example: The migration was completed *ahead of schedule* with *zero downtime*.
Inline Code
Use backticks for `inline code`. Example: Run `kubectl get pods -n production` to check pod status. The config file is at `/etc/azure/config.json`.
Combined Formatting
All three can appear in a single paragraph: **Azure Policy** enforces *organizational standards* using the `azurerm_policy_assignment` resource. Run `az policy assignment list` to verify.
The source_code type renders text in a monospace font with the SourceCode paragraph style. Use for configuration files, scripts, and multi-line code samples.
Terraform Example
Azure CLI Example

az network vnet create \
  --resource-group rg-network-hub \
  --name vnet-hub-eastus2 \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --subnet-name snet-gateway \
  --subnet-prefix 10.0.1.0/24
JSON Configuration
Tables are generated from structured row data. The first row is treated as the header row by default, receiving the table style's header formatting (typically dark background with white text).

rows: Create and manage resources, no RBAC

content.text: Compliance Framework Matrix
Tables with **inline formatting** in cells are supported:
This section demonstrates how all content block types work together in a realistic document section — a network troubleshooting runbook entry.
Connectivity Troubleshooting
When a spoke virtual network loses connectivity to the hub, follow this procedure to diagnose and restore service.
This runbook applies only to hub-and-spoke topologies using Azure Virtual WAN. For classic VNet peering, refer to the Network Peering Runbook.
Diagnostic Steps
Verify the VNet peering status is *Connected* (not *Disconnected* or *Initiated*).
Check Azure Firewall rules for the affected spoke's address range.
Review Network Security Group (NSG) flow logs for denied traffic.
Test DNS resolution from a VM in the spoke network.
Common Causes
**Expired peering** — peering was deleted during a Terraform apply
**NSG rule conflict** — a deny-all rule was added at a higher priority
**DNS misconfiguration** — custom DNS server is unreachable
**Route table override** — a UDR is sending traffic to a black hole
Verification Commands
Run the following from the Azure Cloud Shell or a management VM:

# Check peering status
az network vnet peering list \
  --resource-group rg-network-hub \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-eastus2 \
  --output table

# Check effective routes on the spoke VM NIC
az network nic show-effective-route-table \
  --resource-group rg-workload-prod \
  --name nic-vm-app01 \
  --output table
Escalation Matrix

rows: Cloud Platform Team → Director of Engineering

content.text: After restoring connectivity, document the root cause and update this runbook if a new failure mode was identified.
